meta.title: Mercedes-Benz M103 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M103 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M103 is a robust, simple straight-six — a classic bulletproof workhorse, but head gasket and timing chain guide wear are common. Backed by real UK specialist data [ESTIMATED].

rows.value: Confidence: 80% confidence — based on classic Mercedes specialist and enthusiast consensus, cross-referenced with owner forum reporting.

rows.metric: Simple, accessible SOHC straight-six layout; most repairs are straightforward for any Mercedes specialist

rows.value: Well-supported via classic Mercedes specialists and European suppliers; some parts are classic-only but readily available

rows.metric: Reconditioned cost as % of lowest host value — £2,750 on a £2,000 car = 137% → 4

rows.value: One-line verdict: "The M103 is a famously bulletproof straight-six — but head gaskets and timing chain guides fail with age on higher-mileage examples — buy on condition, not just mileage."

rows.metric: Best For: Classic Mercedes enthusiasts wanting a reliable, torque-rich six with simple, accessible mechanicals.
rows.value: Avoid If: Buyers who need modern fuel economy or aren't prepared for classic-car maintenance and parts-sourcing realities.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M103 typically takes 8–10 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent specialist) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE classic dealer) [ESTIMATED].

cta.label: → Compare M103 engine prices from UK Mercedes classic specialists

items.question: How much does an M103 engine replacement cost?
items.answer: A reconditioned M103 typically costs £2,000–£3,500 supply only, with fitting adding £500–£800 depending on labour rates [ESTIMATED]. Used examples can be found from £500, making it a relatively affordable classic engine replacement.

items.question: Is the M103 a reliable engine?
items.answer: The M103 rates 4.0★ out of 5 — widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most robust and bulletproof engines. Its simple SOHC design and mechanical fuel injection mean fewer electronics to fail; regular oil changes and cooling system care are the keys to longevity.

items.question: What is the most common M103 failure?
items.answer: Head gasket failure is the most common, typically between cylinders or externally at high mileage. Timing chain guide wear (causing rattle on cold start) and distributor cap/rotor corrosion are also well-documented issues on older, high-mileage examples.

items.question: Which Mercedes models use the M103?
items.answer: The M103 powers the W124 E‑Class (260E/300E), W126 S‑Class (260SE/300SE), W201 190E (190E 2.6/3.0), and the W463 G‑Class (G 300) — spanning 1984 to 1995 across four classic Mercedes families.

items.question: How long does the M103 last?
items.answer: With proper maintenance, 250,000–300,000+ miles is achievable. Many well-maintained W124 and W126 examples exceed 300,000 miles on original bottom ends. Cooling system neglect and neglected timing chain guides are the primary causes of premature failure.
